channel.title: Hapi — Happy News by Region
channel.description: Uplifting stories from every corner of the world, organised by region.

item.title: Africa Launches Decade-Long Health Workforce Agenda in Accra
item.description: Health ministers and heads of state gathered in Accra for the 2nd Africa Health Workforce Investment Forum, adopting the 2026–2035 "Plan. Train. Retain." roadmap. Backed by a $4.4 billion World Bank portfolio, the agenda targets creating over 2 million healthcare jobs and closing critical staffing gaps across the continent.

item.title: Ghana’s Cedi Ranks Among Africa’s Best-Performing Currencies
item.description: Ghana’s cedi emerged as one of Africa’s top-performing currencies in April 2026, cementing the country’s economic recovery following years of IMF-backed fiscal reform. Renewed investor confidence and improved macroeconomic stability are driving the turnaround for one of West Africa’s leading economies.

item.title: Kenya Opens World’s Largest Black Rhino Sanctuary in Tsavo West
item.description: Kenya officially opened a 3,200 square kilometre sanctuary in Tsavo West National Park, protecting 200 black rhinos in the largest concentration of the species in the country. President Ruto called the milestone a symbol of Kenya’s commitment to doubling its national rhino population by 2037.

item.title: Filipino Girl Group BINI Wins Billboard Global Force Award
item.description: Eight-member Filipino group BINI made history as the first Southeast Asian act to receive the Billboard Global Force Award, presented at the Hollywood Palladium. Performing in traditional Filipino attire, they dedicated the honour to their fans and declared themselves “proud Filipinas” on the world stage.

item.title: Bangkok Confirmed as Host of Inaugural Eurovision Song Contest Asia
item.description: Bangkok, Thailand was officially named host city for the first-ever Eurovision Song Contest Asia, launching in November 2026. The European Broadcasting Union’s expansion brings the beloved competition format to Asia-Pacific for the first time, with 18 participating countries from across the region.

item.title: South Korean Youth Activist Wins Goldman Environmental Prize for Asia
item.description: 31-year-old Borim Kim, founder of Youth 4 Climate Action, won the 2026 Goldman Environmental Prize for leading the first successful youth climate lawsuit in Asia. Her campaign resulted in South Korea’s Constitutional Court ruling that inadequate climate policies violated citizens’ constitutional rights, mandating binding emissions targets through 2049.

item.title: Amsterdam Bans Fossil Fuel and Meat Ads From All Public Spaces
item.description: Amsterdam became the world’s first capital city to prohibit advertising for fossil fuels and meat products across all public spaces, from bus shelters to city buses and billboards. The policy extends the city’s 2022 fossil fuel ad ban into dietary choices as part of its ambitious climate action plan.

item.title: EU Solar Push Could Halve Electricity Costs and Save €223 Billion by 2030
item.description: A new SolarPower Europe report found that aggressively scaling solar and battery storage across the EU could cut electricity system costs by nearly half before the decade ends, saving €223 billion annually in fossil fuel imports. Clean energy is now both the fastest and cheapest route to European energy security.

item.title: UK Common Cranes Soar to Record Numbers in Historic Breeding Season
item.description: Britain’s population of common cranes reached an all-time high in 2026, with the iconic birds now breeding successfully across wetlands in East Anglia and beyond. The milestone reflects decades of habitat restoration work and ranks as one of the UK’s most celebrated wildlife comeback stories.

item.title: Scientists Find “Holy Grail” Gene That Lets Axolotls Regrow Limbs
item.description: Researchers identified the SP8 transcription factor as a critical driver of limb regeneration in axolotls, and demonstrated it can trigger similar processes in mice, which normally cannot regrow lost tissue. The finding is being hailed as a foundational step toward therapies that could one day help humans recover from traumatic injuries and organ damage.

item.title: Mexico Launches Universal Healthcare for All 120 Million Residents
item.description: President Claudia Sheinbaum signed a historic decree establishing Mexico’s Universal Health Service, committing to free, comprehensive healthcare for every resident by 2027. The programme prioritises underserved rural and Indigenous communities who have historically had the least access to care.

item.title: Endangered Right Whales Celebrate Baby Boom With 23 Calves This Season
item.description: Scientists documented 23 North Atlantic right whale calves this season—double last year’s count and the highest tally in 17 years for one of the world’s most endangered marine mammals. The baby boom brings cautious hope for a species with only around 380 individuals remaining.

item.title: Brazil Welcomes Record 9.3 Million Visitors in Q1 2026, Tourism Up 37%
item.description: Brazil recorded its best-ever first quarter for international tourism, welcoming 9.3 million visitors—a 37% jump over the previous year. Argentina surpassed the US as Brazil’s top source market, reflecting strengthened regional ties and surging demand for Brazil’s cultural festivals, beaches, and ecotourism experiences.

item.title: Lula and Trump Declare Brazil-US Relations “Stabilized” After White House Summit
item.description: Brazilian President Lula and President Trump emerged from a three-hour White House meeting with an agreement to resolve trade tensions within 30 days. The breakthrough—linked to US interest in Brazil’s vast critical minerals reserves—signals a new chapter for hemispheric economic cooperation.

item.title: Real-Time Payments to Bring 1.1 Million Argentines Into the Banking System
item.description: A new report by the Centre for Economics and Business Research found real-time payment networks will add $19.3 billion to Argentina’s GDP by 2028 and bring 1.1 million previously unbanked people into the formal financial system. Peru and Chile are set to see similar gains as Latin America’s fintech revolution expands.

item.title: Niue Elects Record Seven Women to Parliament, Crossing UN Gender Threshold
item.description: The tiny Pacific island of Niue made history by electing seven women to its 20-seat parliament, achieving 35% female representation and crossing the UN’s “critical mass” threshold for gender equity in governance. Female representation in Pacific parliaments typically sits below 10%, making Niue’s result a landmark for the region.

item.title: Pacific Resilience Facility Enters Into Force as Fiji Completes Ratification
item.description: The Pacific Resilience Facility Treaty officially came into force on May 6 after Fiji and Australia deposited ratification instruments in Suva. Described as the first Pacific-led climate financing facility, it provides grant funding for community resilience projects, clean energy, and climate adaptation across island nations, with a target of $1.5 billion.

item.title: Tonga Establishes Integrated Governance Over 700,000 km² of Ocean Territory
item.description: Tonga gazetted its landmark Ocean Management Act, establishing comprehensive, integrated governance over its vast 700,000 square kilometre marine territory—one of the most ambitious ocean stewardship frameworks in the Pacific. The law consolidates fisheries, environment, and maritime regulations under a single framework to protect Tonga’s rich marine biodiversity.

item.title: Antarctic Voyage Maps 400 Deep-Sea Species Near Unexplored Cook Ice Shelf
item.description: Australia’s CSIRO research vessel RV Investigator completed the COOKIES expedition, documenting over 400 deep-sea species and capturing more than 10,000 images from the rarely visited Cook Ice Shelf marine region. The voyage delivered the most detailed survey of life in these icy depths ever conducted, opening a new window into Antarctic biodiversity.

item.title: West Africa Launches Bold Roadmap for Universal Health Coverage
item.description: Ministers of health and finance from 24 Western and Central African countries gathered in Accra to adopt the World Bank's Fit to Prosper health strategy. The initiative targets universal health coverage for 200 million people by 2030, backed by sustained regional investment and strengthened community health systems.

item.title: Second Set of Rare Gorilla Twins Born at Virunga in 2026
item.description: For the second time in three months, twin mountain gorillas were born at DRC's Virunga National Park — among the rarest births in the primate world. The Baraka family now numbers 19 individuals, and 9 new gorilla babies have arrived at Virunga in the first three months of 2026 alone.

item.title: Canon and Oceans Alive Restore Coral Reefs Along Kenya's Coast
item.description: Canon Central and North Africa has partnered with Kenya's Oceans Alive Foundation to scale up coral nursery propagation and women-led reef rehabilitation along the country's northern coastline. The initiative builds on a decade of work that already recorded a 300% rise in fish biomass within protected zones.

item.title: Japan Becomes First G7 Nation to Allow Joint Child Custody
item.description: Japan's revised Civil Code took effect April 1, allowing divorced parents to negotiate shared parental authority for the first time in the country's history. The landmark reform ends Japan's status as the only G7 nation with mandatory sole custody, and introduces a statutory minimum child-support system.

item.title: South Korea's Baeksang Awards Break Ground With New Musical Division
item.description: The 62nd Baeksang Arts Awards made history on May 8 by establishing a new Musical Division in partnership with the Korea Musical Theatre Association, marking 60 years of Korean musical theatre. Hyun Bin and Park Bo Young took top honors, while the ceremony drew global audiences celebrating the depth of Korean storytelling.

item.title: BOE and UNESCO Launch Asia-Pacific Science Club Network
item.description: BOE Technology and UNESCO unveiled a new Asia-Pacific Science Club Network and 2026 Science Challenge, uniting 40 Chinese teachers with 15 educators from 12 Asia-Pacific countries. Launched under the UN's International Decade of Sciences for Sustainable Development, it will connect young scientists across the region for collaborative hands-on projects.

item.title: The World Celebrates David Attenborough's Remarkable 100th Birthday
item.description: Sir David Attenborough turned 100 on May 8, drawing tributes from King Charles III, Leonardo DiCaprio, and scientists who named a new wasp genus in his honor. LEGO updated its age range to 4-100+, post boxes across the UK were adorned with crocheted wildlife, and the Royal Albert Hall hosted a centenary concert.

item.title: Thames Reborn: River Now Supports 115 Fish Species After Decades of Recovery
item.description: The tidal Thames now hosts over 115 fish species, 92 bird species, and nearly 600 hectares of saltmarsh — a stunning turnaround for a river declared biologically dead in 1957. The Zoological Society of London's annual health check confirms that seals, seahorses, and even sharks are now regular residents.

item.title: France Bans Forever Chemicals From Cosmetics and Clothing
item.description: France became the world's first country to ban PFAS from cosmetics, clothing, and ski wax where alternatives exist, with restrictions now fully in force. The law also mandates drinking-water testing for PFAS contamination and fines industrial polluters — setting a bold new global standard for protecting human health.

item.title: US Job Growth Nearly Doubles Forecasts With 115,000 April Additions
item.description: The US economy added 115,000 jobs in April 2026, nearly double the analyst forecast of 65,000, while unemployment held steady at 4.3%. The stronger-than-expected result signals continued labor-market resilience despite global trade uncertainty, offering a lift to workers and businesses across North America.

item.title: NBA Pioneer Jason Collins Wins Inaugural Bill Walton Champion Award
item.description: Jason Collins — who made history in 2014 as the first openly gay active athlete in any major US professional sports league — received the inaugural Bill Walton Global Champion Award at the Green Sports Alliance Summit on May 6. The award honors athletes who use their platform to drive positive social and environmental change.

item.title: Peabody Awards Honor Documentaries on Sly Stone and Fela Kuti
item.description: The 86th Peabody Awards celebrated outstanding storytelling, with programs about music legends Sly Stone and Fela Kuti among the honored works. Special awards recognized Amy Poehler's career achievement, screenwriter Sterlin Harjo as a trailblazer, and PBS KIDS as a historic institution — honoring media that uplifts and connects communities.

item.title: Brazil Positions Itself as Critical Minerals Leader With New Strategy
item.description: Brazil's lower chamber voted to create a National Critical Minerals Strategy, establishing incentives for extraction and processing of the country's vast reserves of lithium, cobalt, and rare earths. The move positions Brazil as a cornerstone supplier for the global clean-energy transition, turning natural wealth into long-term economic opportunity.

item.title: Latin America's Economy Set to Grow 2.1% in 2026 Despite Headwinds
item.description: Despite global trade disruptions, Latin America is on course for 2.1% GDP growth in 2026, building on a resilient prior year. Analysts credit diversified export markets and deepening regional trade links for sustaining momentum — a signal of growing economic strength across the continent.

item.title: Brazil's Cattle Ranchers Become Unexpected Heroes of Pantanal Conservation
item.description: Ranchers in Brazil's Pantanal — the world's largest tropical wetland — are partnering with Pew Charitable Trusts conservationists to protect biodiversity alongside their herds. The unlikely alliance is showing that sustainable ranching and thriving wildlife can coexist, offering a replicable model for conservation across agricultural landscapes.

item.title: New Zealand's Kākāpō Parrots Hatch 100+ Chicks in Record Breeding Year
item.description: New Zealand's critically endangered kākāpō parrots have had their best breeding season on record, with more than 100 chicks hatching across three island sanctuaries. The flightless parrots only breed when rimu trees mass-fruit every few years, making 2026's bumper result a genuine conservation triumph for this beloved species.

item.title: New Zealand Archers Sweep All Medals at Home Oceania Championships
item.description: New Zealand's archers delivered a dominant performance on home soil at the 2026 World Archery Oceania Target Championships in Auckland, sweeping the recurve individual podiums in both the men's and women's events. The clean-sweep victory lit up home crowds and reflected years of sustained investment in the sport.

item.title: Inside Coral Reefs: 645 New Microbial Species Mapped in Pacific Survey
item.description: Researchers from the University of Galway reconstructed genomes of 645 microbial species from 99 Pacific coral reefs, finding over 99% had never been genetically described. Published in Nature on May 8, the study reveals a vast pharmaceutical library hidden inside coral ecosystems — one that could yield future life-saving medicines.

item.title: Scientists Find Earth's Oldest Air in 6-Million-Year-Old Antarctic Ice
item.description: Researchers from the Center for Oldest Ice Exploration discovered 6-million-year-old ice in East Antarctica's Allan Hills, more than doubling the previous record for the oldest dated ice. Tiny air bubbles sealed within offer a direct window into Earth's ancient atmosphere, yielding the first direct measurements of how much Antarctica has cooled over millions of years.

item.title: South Africa Set for Record Clean Energy Investment in 2026
item.description: South Africa is on track for a record 5,252 MW of renewable energy and battery storage investment closures in 2026 — the highest single-year total in its history. Eight projects worth 1,932 MW had already closed by April 30 with 26 more in the pipeline. The milestone also coincides with over 300 consecutive days without power blackouts.

item.title: Artscape Theatre Cape Town Marks 55 Years as a Place for All
item.description: Cape Town's Artscape Theatre is celebrating 55 years with the theme 'A Place for All,' honouring its transformation from a racially segregated apartheid venue to one of Africa's most inclusive performing arts centres. Free school concerts and a community photograph on May 19 open a year-long celebration, closing with a gala in November.

item.title: Cape Vultures Return to South Africa's Eastern Cape After 30 Years
item.description: Eighty-five Cape Vultures were spotted on an Eastern Cape farm — the first confirmed sighting in over 30 years — the result of Vulpro's decades of captive breeding and veterinary care with a 75% survival rate. The species has been downlisted from Endangered to Vulnerable, and conservation safe zones are being standardised across Southern Africa.

item.title: India and Japan Sign Landmark Quantum Science and Health Pacts
item.description: India and Japan signed a Letter of Intent on Quantum Science and Technology cooperation and a health and medical devices memorandum, linking India's National Quantum Mission with Japan's world-leading research institutions. The agreements include expanded researcher exchange programmes and joint innovation platforms, deepening ties between Asia's two technology powerhouses.

item.title: Another Vietnamese Province Becomes Free of Bear Bile Farming
item.description: Animals Asia announced that another Vietnamese province has become free of bear bile farming after the rescue of Misa, a moon bear held captive for over 25 years. Vietnam's commitment to end bile farming by 2026 has driven bear numbers in captivity from 4,300 in 2005 to around 200 today — a 95% reduction.

item.title: Malaysia Sets Southeast Asia Record with 10 Million Q1 Tourists
item.description: Malaysia welcomed over 10 million international visitors in Q1 2026 — a record-breaking first and the highest quarterly total in Southeast Asia. Chinese arrivals rose 22.7% year-on-year, fuelled by Malaysia's expanded visa-free policies and improved air connectivity, placing it ahead of Thailand and Vietnam as the region's most-visited country for the quarter.

item.title: UK Bowel Cancer Trial Shows Zero Relapses in Three Years With Immunotherapy
item.description: All 32 patients in the NEOPRISM-CRC trial who received nine weeks of pembrolizumab immunotherapy for bowel cancer before surgery have remained cancer-free for nearly three years — zero relapses. Presented at AACR 2026, the results challenge the standard surgery-first model and could transform treatment for one of the world's most common cancers.

item.title: Scotland's Wildcats Are Thriving in Cairngorms With Wild Litters Confirmed
item.description: Scotland's wildcat reintroduction programme has reached a historic milestone, with at least nine litters confirmed born in Cairngorms National Park since releases began in 2023. Once considered functionally extinct for decades, breeding pairs are firmly established — and four kittens bred in 2025 are ready for 2026 release.

item.title: V&A East Museum Opens in London's Olympic Park — Free for All
item.description: The V&A East Museum opened in London's Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park, bringing 500+ objects from the Victoria & Albert collection to East London through free permanent galleries. Its inaugural exhibition, 'The Music is Black: A British Story,' traces 125 years of Black British music, anchoring a major cultural regeneration of the 2012 Olympic site.

item.title: Pancreatic Cancer Drug Daraxonrasib Nearly Doubles Survival in NEJM Trial
item.description: Revolution Medicines published NEJM data showing daraxonrasib nearly doubled median survival for metastatic pancreatic cancer patients — 15.6 months versus 6.7 months on chemotherapy. Since 90% of pancreatic cancers carry RAS mutations, this targeted therapy could benefit the vast majority of the 60,000 Americans diagnosed each year, with an FDA fast-track application already filed.

item.title: Injectable Biomaterial Heals Heart Attack Tissue Through the Bloodstream
item.description: UC San Diego engineers developed an injectable biomaterial that travels through the bloodstream to repair heart attack damage from within — no surgery, just an IV. Tested successfully in both rodents and large animals, it reduces inflammation and jumpstarts tissue repair. Human trials are expected to begin within two years.

item.title: Green Sea Turtle Upgraded From Endangered to Least Concern by IUCN
item.description: The green sea turtle has been reclassified from Endangered to Least Concern on the IUCN Red List, following a 28% rebound in global nesting populations. Decades of protected beaches, turtle excluder devices in fishing nets, and reduced poaching drove the recovery — with Florida nesting sites up 134% since 1984.

item.title: Peru's Indigenous-Led Amazon Reserve Protects Two-Thirds of Its Freshwater Fish
item.description: Peru's 397,000-acre Bajo Putumayo Yaguas Communal Reserve — co-managed by six Indigenous peoples including the Bora, Yagua, and Ticuna — is being consolidated in 2026, sheltering 65% of Peru's freshwater fish and a third of its mammal species. The result of decades of Indigenous-led advocacy, it anchors a biocultural corridor linking Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, and Brazil.

item.title: Colombia's Deforestation Falls to Second-Lowest Level Since 2016
item.description: Colombia's primary forest loss fell 17% from 2024 to 2025, hitting its second-lowest level since 2016 and beating national targets ahead of schedule. Stronger enforcement, ministerial coordination, and community conservation agreements under the Peace Agreement all contributed — offering real hope for the country's long-embattled forests.

item.title: Peru Tourism Grows 3.5% in Q1 2026, Chile Becomes Top Source Market
item.description: Peru welcomed 823,863 international visitors in Q1 2026 — a 3.5% year-on-year rise — with Chile overtaking the United States as Peru's top tourist source market for the first time. South American regional arrivals now account for 53% of inbound tourism, reflecting surging intra-continental travel and a sustained post-pandemic recovery.

item.title: Sunlight Turns Plastic Waste Into Clean Hydrogen in Adelaide Lab Breakthrough
item.description: University of Adelaide researchers developed a solar-driven process that converts waste plastic into clean hydrogen fuel and industrial chemicals using only sunlight and photocatalysts, running continuously for over 100 hours. More energy-efficient than standard water-splitting, the technique addresses the plastic pollution crisis and the clean energy transition in one elegant step.

item.title: Australia Wins Its First-Ever World Curling Championship in Geneva
item.description: Tahli Gill and Dean Hewitt made history by winning Australia's first-ever World Curling Championship, defeating Sweden 8–4 in the Mixed Doubles final in Geneva. Going 8–1 in round-robin play and beating defending champions Italy in the semis, they brought home gold in a sport where Australia had never before reached the top of the podium.

item.title: New Zealand Kiwi Returns to Wellington After More Than a Century Away
item.description: The Capital Kiwi Project translocated its 250th kiwi to the hills surrounding Wellington — the first time New Zealand's national bird has been established in the capital for over a century. Seven birds were ceremonially carried through Parliament before their release, and the project reports a remarkable 90% chick survival rate.

item.title: Antarctic Cruises Become Live Science Missions in WHOI-Ponant Partnership
item.description: Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution and cruise line Ponant have partnered to embed scientists aboard Antarctic expedition voyages, collecting glacier laser measurements and high-resolution imagery that satellites cannot reach. The three-year programme turns expedition cruises into live research platforms — with passengers joining onboard lectures and lab tours as the science unfolds.
